title: Relight my fire? Why ‘reindustrialisation’ needs a rethink
gist: Resolution Foundation argues UK 'reindustrialisation' nostalgia is misguided—Manchester's shift to knowledge-intensive services beat every other city on incomes—while flagging severe long-run fiscal risks (net debt at 300% of GDP by 2075, AI eroding the tax base) and the material costs of unaffordable housing.

insights.claim: Manufacturing jobs have declined for decades across the western world and no politician has reversed the automation and globalisation driving the fall, making nostalgic 'reindustrialisation' pledges unlikely to restore factory employment.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Discount UK political promises to revive manufacturing employment; the durable regional-growth trade is services, not factories.
insights.quote: manufacturing jobs have been declining for decades across the western world, and no politician yet has turned back the clock on the automation and globalisation that have driven this fall

insights.claim: Manchester's manufacturing now accounts for fewer than one-in-twelve jobs, while knowledge-intensive business services (scientists, engineers, software developers) have almost doubled since the late 1990s and now provide more than twice as many jobs as manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: The proven UK regional-revival template is KIBS clustering, not industrial policy—relevant for siting and place-based investment.
insights.quote: the sector now accounts for fewer than one-in-twelve jobs

insights.claim: Manchester's services-led reinvention did not cost living standards: its Gross Disposable Household Income grew faster than any other major UK city since 1997, undercutting the assumption that losing manufacturing means falling incomes.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Evidence that deindustrialisation plus services growth can raise incomes strengthens the case for services-first regional policy.
insights.quote: Gross Disposable Household Incomes (GDHI) grew over this period, when Manchester performed better than any other major city since 1997

insights.claim: Resolution Foundation reframes 'reindustrialisation' to mean building knowledge-intensive services rather than reviving factories, matching the strategy from its own Economy 2030 Inquiry.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Watch whether Andy Burnham's agenda adopts a services-redefinition of reindustrialisation—a signal of credible vs symbolic regional policy.

insights.claim: A stocktake by Ed Balls, Anna Stansbury and Dan Turner concluded the Starmer government's efforts on regional inequality still fall 'well short of the scale, balance and ambition required' to reverse long-standing spatial inequalities.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Regional-inequality policy remains under-delivered under Starmer—room for a bigger fiscal or devolution announcement later.
insights.quote: well short of the scale, balance and ambition required…to reverse long-standing spatial inequalities
insights.locator: Chart of the week considers this shift

insights.claim: Lancashire ranks mid-table on Northern productivity but above the UK average, yet its manufacturing GVA gains came from aerospace machinery made in Preston—a specialist sector that delivered no real jobs boost, while deprivation persists in Blackpool, Blackburn and Burnley.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: High-value specialist manufacturing can lift GVA without creating jobs—caution against headline output figures as a jobs proxy.

insights.claim: The OBR projects UK public sector net debt reaching 300 per cent of GDP by 2075 on unchanged policy, driven by rising health and care spending for an aging population with no funding plan in place.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: A structural gilt-supply and fiscal-sustainability risk that underpins the case for higher-for-longer UK term premia.
insights.quote: public sector net debt at 300 per cent of GDP by 2075
insights.locator: Something for the weekend | Risky business

insights.claim: The OBR baseline has UK health spending rising from 8 per cent of GDP in 2030-31 to 13 per cent by 2075-76, and state pension spending from 5 per cent to 9 per cent over the same period.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Quantifies the demographic spending wedge—roughly 9 points of GDP—that future UK tax or spending policy must close.
insights.quote: health spending will rise from 8 per cent of GDP in 2030-31 to 13 per cent in 2075-76
insights.locator: The OBR has to warn of this debt-pocalypse

insights.claim: OBR analysis shows the UK is in its worst era for productivity growth since the 1800s, framing the productivity slump as historically unprecedented rather than merely cyclical.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Persistently weak productivity caps trend growth and tax revenue, reinforcing the fiscal-sustainability bear case.
insights.quote: it shows we’re in the worst era for productivity growth since the 1800s
insights.locator: You can always rely on the OBR

insights.claim: The OBR illustrates that if AI reduces the labour share of the economy, it could cut government tax receipts by up to 4 percentage points of GDP versus baseline in one scenario, because the UK tax system leans heavily on taxing labour.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: AI-driven capital-labour shifts are a structural threat to labour-based tax bases—watch for tax-system redesign toward capital/consumption.
insights.quote: if AI reduces the labour share in the economy it could be huge(ly bad) for government tax revenues

insights.claim: Nick Bloom and colleagues estimate the firing of the US Bureau of Labour Statistics Commissioner cost roughly $20 billion off GDP, attributing 9 per cent of the subsequent jump in the Economic Policy Uncertainty Index—and the associated hiring and investment hit—to the sacking.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Politicising official statistics carries a measurable growth cost—an underappreciated macro risk from institutional erosion.
insights.quote: $20 billion, according to Nick Bloom and colleagues

insights.claim: US policy uncertainty averaged nearly three times its historical level in 2025 versus two-and-a-half times for the UK, yet UK policy uncertainty in this Parliament has been higher than in any of the previous seven.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Elevated policy uncertainty on both sides of the Atlantic is a persistent drag on business investment—relevant for capex-sensitive positioning.
insights.quote: its index averaged nearly three times its historical level in 2025, against two-and-a-half times for the UK that year

themes.theme: Housing affordability and its material costs

insights.claim: Across EU27 countries from 2010 to 2023, housing costs just 1 per cent above the EU average were associated with overcrowding 2.3 per cent higher, severe housing deprivation 1.4 per cent higher, labour force participation 0.55 per cent lower, poverty 0.9 per cent higher and 'bad' self-reported health 1 per cent higher.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Quantifies how unaffordable housing depresses labour supply and health—evidence base for housing as a macro (not just social) lever.

insights.claim: Resolution Foundation argues housing affordability can only be fixed long-term through housebuilding, while the near-term remedy is linking support to the actual cost of rents.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Signals RF's policy push—rent-linked support now, supply later—worth tracking for UK housing and benefits policy direction.
insights.quote: In the long run, we can’t address this without housebuilding.
